_metadata.notes: Curated historical/economic region memberships for the US choropleth overlay. State-level approximations of regions that are mostly defined sub-state in reality (a state is in 'Appalachia' if any sizable part of its territory is, etc.). Edit this file when better definitions surface; nothing fetches it. The federally-defined regions (Appalachia per ARC) are taken from the ARC; the cultural/historical ones (Rust Belt, Sun Belt, Black Belt, Tech Corridors) are conventional usage with some states arguably borderline.

rust_belt.label: Rust Belt
rust_belt.description: Mid-Atlantic and Midwest states whose 20th-century manufacturing base (steel, autos, glass, machinery) declined sharply post-1970. Population stagnant or shrinking; rents reflect weak demand + abundant older housing stock.

sun_belt.label: Sun Belt
sun_belt.description: Southern + southwestern states with rapid post-1970 population growth driven by air-conditioning, retiree migration, lower taxes, and (more recently) tech/financial-services relocations from coastal high-cost states. Rents rising fast on supply pressure.

appalachia.label: Appalachia (ARC)
appalachia.description: Federal Appalachian Regional Commission definition — mountain states whose economies historically depended on coal, timber, and tobacco. Persistently lower median income; cheap land and housing but limited employment.

tech_corridors.label: Tech corridors
tech_corridors.description: States with major tech-industry concentrations: Silicon Valley + LA (CA), Seattle (WA), Boston-128 (MA), Austin (TX), Research Triangle (NC), Denver-Boulder (CO), Northern Virginia (VA), NYC fintech/adtech (NY). Wage premiums + housing supply constraints drive top-quartile rents.

great_plains.label: Great Plains
great_plains.description: Wheat-belt and ranching states with sparse population, wind-energy boom, and agriculture-dominant economies. Cheap rents, slow population growth.

oil_patch.label: Oil Patch
oil_patch.description: States with major oil + gas production: Permian Basin (TX, NM), Bakken (ND), Anadarko (OK), Wyoming, Alaska's North Slope, Louisiana Gulf, Kansas. Rents in extraction-county boomtowns spike when oil prices rise (Williston ND, Midland TX) but the state-average effect is modest.

black_belt.label: Black Belt
black_belt.description: Crescent of rural Deep-South counties named for the historically dark fertile cotton soil; high African-American population share, persistent poverty, slow-growth agricultural and small-manufacturing economies. Cheap rents but limited opportunity.

new_england.label: New England
new_england.description: Six original colonial states. Mature service economies (higher ed, finance, healthcare, biotech), aging demographics, restrictive zoning + old housing stock. Rents middle-to-high.
